text.title: Card Sorting — 6-Step Process
text.summary: Structured procedure for running open, closed, or hybrid card sorts to reveal user mental models for content organization.

section.title: Step 1: Define Goals

rule.statement: State the IA decision the sort will inform before selecting card set or format.
rule.rationale: Card sorting produces data; the decision it must drive determines what questions to ask, how many cards to include, and whether to run open or closed format.

section.p: Goal examples: How do users group our content? What labels make sense to users? Does our current navigation match mental models?

rule.statement: Use 30-60 cards written in user language — no internal jargon, no system terminology, representative of the full content set.
rule.rationale: Over 60 cards causes cognitive fatigue that degrades sort quality. Jargon on cards tests vocabulary knowledge rather than mental models. Below 30 cards under-represents the content space.

rule.statement: Use open sort for early exploration (users create categories); closed sort for validation (30+ participants needed for statistical reliability); hybrid when refining an existing IA.
rule.rationale: Open sort reveals what categories users create naturally; closed sort tests whether users can place content in predefined categories. Using closed sort for exploration misses the labels users would naturally use.

rule.statement: Recruit 15-20 participants for open sort; 30+ for closed sort. Match target user profile.
rule.rationale: Open sort patterns emerge at 15+ participants. Closed sort requires 30+ for the placement percentages to be statistically meaningful. Fewer participants produce unreliable similarity matrices.

section.title: Step 5: Conduct Sessions

rule.statement: Give standard instructions without examples of how to group — examples bias grouping decisions.
rule.rationale: If the instructions include a sample grouping ("for example, you might group these together"), participants anchor on that example and produce less authentic mental models.

section.p: Open sort: "Please organize these cards into groups that make sense to you. You can create as many groups as you need. When done, give each group a name."
Closed sort: "Please place each card into one of these categories. If a card doesn't fit anywhere, put it in 'Doesn't Fit'."

section.title: Step 6: Analyze Results

rule.statement: Use similarity matrix thresholds to determine grouping confidence: above 70% = definite grouping; 40-70% = consider grouping; below 40% = keep separate.
rule.rationale: Without thresholds, analysts interpret matrices subjectively. Fixed thresholds make the decision rule explicit and repeatable across analysts.

list.item: Agreement rate — how often items were grouped together across participants.
Category similarity — overlap between user-created and predefined categories.
Standardization — consistency of category structures across participants (high = clear mental model exists).

section.reference: Results report template with agreement matrix
